content: Facilitate a meeting. List all the needs on a board. Everyone gets to choose their top two priorities. After the other needs have been eliminated, each stakeholder votes on their top choice. The intent is to ensure all parties are heard and that they have input into the outcome. The business analyst ensures that the outcome aligns with the business need.
feedback: This is an invented variation on Compromise, where no one gets everything they want, but where they have input and get some of the things that are important to them. However, not only does the BABOK not mention this process, but it does not promote a specific process on how to perform the underlying competencies. This is too specific to be a realistic answer.

content: Facilitate several meetings to discuss the differences among the stakeholders, ensure that they have an opportunity to express their feelings and needs, ensure an outcome that satisfies the needs of the stakeholder and aligns with the business need. The intent is to strengthen the relationships among the affected stakeholders.
feedback: BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills suggest that it will probably take multiple meetings to have all parties be heard and have their feelings and needs expressed.

content: Talk to the stakeholders individually. Ensure that you understand the real need behind the stated need. Have a meeting with the sponsor to discuss these needs. Encourage the sponsor to talk to the stakeholders and get them on board. The intent is to ensure all parties are heard.
feedback: The BABOK does not recognize this as an effective technique. BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills. A key goal of negotiation and resolving conflict is to ensure that it strengthens the relationships among the different stakeholders. This process does not accomplish that goal.

content: Talk to the sponsor, since the sponsor makes the ultimate decision about priorities. Set up a meeting to communicate the sponsor's decision to the affected stakeholders. This conflict-resolution technique is used when the initiative is stuck when there are tight timeframes, and when not having a decision would delay the initiative. The intent is to ensure that development of the solution is not delayed and that all parties can move forward.
feedback: Forcing a decision will resolve the conflict, but it does not identify underlying interests of all the parties and will not strengthen relationships among the stakeholders. The BABOK does not recognize forcing as an effective conflict resolution technique. BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills.

content: Analyze the performance measures to provide insights into the performance of the solution in relation to the value it brings. Show Daniel that getting the project done in 6 months, although it presents risks, gives the greatest value to the organization.

Negotiation is needed to reach a common understanding, in this case about which has higher priority, the project or operational work. In addition, this kind of a meeting facilitates stakeholder communication and promotes stakeholder support of the proposed changes (BABOK 9.5 introduction).

A focus group is a means to elicit ideas and opinions about a specific product, service, or opportunity in an interactive group environment; participants are not anonymous. A State Model shows the various states and transformations that an entity might go through like an Account or Statement. It focuses on one entity at a time, and the case study refers to multiple entities: Customer, Account, Location, and Statement. A Data Model would tie all of those together. BABOK 10.44.2

To provide needed flexibility in managing accounts and statements, a data model is essential for structuring the data needed to accomplish it. Given the business rules and relationships presented in the case study, a data model is needed to capture them. “It visually represents the elements that are important to the business, the attributes associated with those elements, and the significant relationships among them. Data models are frequently used in elicitation and requirements analysis and Design.” BABOK 10.15.2

User stories are used “to define features of value to a stakeholder using short, simple documentation. They can serve as a basis for identifying needs and allow for the prioritizing, estimating, and planning of solutions.” Given the solution features are already known, these would not be as helpful for modeling the requirements for the features. BABOK 10.48.2

This is a potentially useful tool that “illustrates the movement and transformation of data between externals (entities) and processes.” It does not depict the relationships of one entity to another, though, so cannot capture the business rules like a data model can. A flexible data model is required for the DFD to reference, so this answer is not the best. BABOK 10.13.2

Very close answer, since there is similarity between a Concept Model and Data Dictionary. The latter typically includes data-specific details that are not present in the case study. “A data dictionary is used to document standard definitions of data elements, their meanings, and allowable values. A data dictionary contains definitions of each data element and indicates how those elements combine into composite data elements.” BABOK 10.12.2

The case study does provide “a list of terms in a particular domain with definitions for those terms and their common synonyms” as a Glossary should do. It includes much more than definitions, though, and better matches the description of a Concept Model. BABOK 10.23.3, 10.11.2

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Concept models can be effective where:… the initiative needs to capture large numbers of business rules…” BABOK 10.11.2

It is true that if there is documentation available to review like in the case study, reviewing it involves document analysis. It is a technique, not a deliverable, and much broader than the more specific answer of Concept Model. BABOK 10.18.1

Given the list of statement options and the combinations, a table could clearly show when a resulting fee would be charged. “Decision tables generally contain one or more condition columns that map to specific data elements, as well as one or more action or outcome columns.” BABOK 10.17.3.1

A CRUD Matrix would not be suitable because those matrices show “which user roles have permission to access specific information entities“ and “…can be used to show which processes, instead of users, have the create, read, update and delete rights.” BABOK Glossary

This could be chosen, but the rules and choices are not complex enough to warrant it in this case. “A decision tree is a method of assessing the preferred outcome where multiple sources of uncertainty may exist.” BABOK 10.16.3.3, 10.17.3
Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

This answer omits the $75,000 installation cost in the benefits minus cost. ((80000+110000+180000)-200,000) / 275,000 = .62, which is incorrect.
This answer represents the net of the benefits minus the expected costs. ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) = 95,000. It is only part of the calculation for the Return on Investment.

The formula in the BABOK for Return on Investment is “((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment).” The numbers in the scenario are: ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) / 275000, which equals .35. Please note the BABOK has some inconsistency in how they define ROI. Their formula above differs from their word explanation, which is: “the net benefits divided by the cost of the change.” “Cost of the change” could be construed as just costs to create and implement a solution, but “Cost of the Investment” would include ongoing costs to support a solution (Total Cost of Ownership), so we suggest using the formula to calculate ROI. Cost of investment is considered to include Total Cost of Ownership and A = $1,275,000 and B = $900,000. Dividing the net benefits by the total investment cost yields ROI of .18 for A and .19 for B. Capacity is 80,000 subscribers given 20,000 = 25% of capacity. Year 1: 20,000 + 10,000 (50%) growth = 30,000. Year 2: 30,000 + 15,000 = 45,000. Year 3: 45,000 + 22,500 = 67,500, which is still under capacity.

Capacity is 80,000 subscribers given 20,000 = 25% of capacity. Year 1: 20,000 + 10,000 (50% growth) growth = 30,000. Year 2: 30,000 + 15,000 = 45,000. Year 3: 45,000 + 22,500 = 67,500. Year 4: 67,500 + 33,750 = 101,250, which is over capacity before year 5.
Capacity is 80,000 subscribers given 20,000 = 25% of capacity. Year 1: 20,000 + 10,000 (50%) growth = 30,000. Year 2: 30,000 + 15,000 = 45,000, which is still under capacity.

Capacity is 80,000 subscribers given that 20,000 = 25% of capacity. (20,000 * 4 = 80,000). Year 1: 20,000 + 10,000 (50%) growth = 30,000. Year 2: 30,000 + 15,000 = 45,000. Year 3: 45,000 + 22,500 = 67,500. Year 4: 67,500 + 33,750 = 101,250. (Note: you can also multiply each year's subscriber total times 1.5 to calculate 50% growth.) The subscriber total exceeds the 80,000 capacity sometime in year 4, making this the correct answer. Capacity is part of "Scalability," an element of Non-Functional Requirements. BABOK 10.30.3.1

content: Determine the measures that can be used to evaluate the effectiveness of the software package the CEO wants.
feedback: Rather than “jumping to a solution” which is what the CEO is doing, the BABOK is clear that current solution performance should be measured. Part of that measurement is to assess “how it aligns with enterprise goals and objectives.” The measurements already cited in the case reflect that the current “solution” in place does not support the company’s goals and objectives. Measuring them is not the best answer. BABOK 8.1.

content: Estimate the potential value for the PAS option to establish whether it is appropriate to meet the company's needs.

content: Interpret and synthesize the performance measures already reported to analyze the value of the current solution before replacing it.
feedback: There are several solution performance measurements already in the case study. The BA would be wise to use them and start analyzing them, especially since the CEO feels strongly about a solution. It could be argued that additional measurements are needed, but the analysis should point out if additional measurements are needed. BABOK 8.2.

content: Develop a change strategy to justify why the software package is the best approach.

However, the CEO's desire for a software package represents stated requirements more than a future state. BABOK 6.4.2

content: Facilitate several meetings to discuss the differences among the stakeholders, ensure that they have an opportunity to express their feelings and needs, ensure an outcome that satisfies the needs of the stakeholder and aligns with the business need. content: Facilitate a meeting. List all the needs on a board. Everyone gets to choose their top two priorities. After the other needs have been eliminated, each stakeholder votes on their top choice. feedback: This is an invented variation on Compromise, where no one gets everything they want, but where they have input and get some of the things that are important to them. content: Talk to the sponsor, since the sponsor makes the ultimate decision about priorities. Set up a meeting to communicate the sponsor's decision to the affected stakeholders. This conflict-resolution technique is used when the initiative is stuck when there are tight timeframes, and when not having a decision would delay the initiative. The intent is to ensure that development of the solution is not delayed and that all parties can move forward.
feedback: Forcing a decision will resolve the conflict, but it does not identify underlying interests of all the parties and will not strengthen relationships among the stakeholders. A State Model shows the various states and transformations that an entity might go through like an Account or Statement. It focuses on one entity at a time, and the case study refers to multiple entities: Customer, Account, Location, and Statement. A Data Model would tie all of those together. BABOK 10.44.2

User stories are used “to define features of value to a stakeholder using short, simple documentation. They can serve as a basis for identifying needs and allow for the prioritizing, estimating, and planning of solutions.” Given the solution features are already known, these would not be as helpful for modeling the requirements for the features. BABOK 10.48.2

To provide needed flexibility in managing accounts and statements, a data model is essential for structuring the data needed to accomplish it. Given the business rules and relationships presented in the case study, a data model is needed to capture them. “It visually represents the elements that are important to the business, the attributes associated with those elements, and the significant relationships among them. Data models are frequently used in elicitation and requirements analysis and Design.” BABOK 10.15.2

This is a potentially useful tool that “illustrates the movement and transformation of data between externals (entities) and processes.” It does not depict the relationships of one entity to another, though, so cannot capture the business rules like a data model can. A flexible data model is required for the DFD to reference, so this answer is not the best. BABOK 10.13.2

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Concept models can be effective where:… the initiative needs to capture large numbers of business rules…” BABOK 10.11.2

Very close answer, since there is similarity between a Concept Model and Data Dictionary. The latter typically includes data-specific details that are not present in the case study. “A data dictionary is used to document standard definitions of data elements, their meanings, and allowable values. A data dictionary contains definitions of each data element and indicates how those elements combine into composite data elements.” BABOK 10.12.2

It is true that if there is documentation available to review like in the case study, reviewing it involves document analysis. It is a technique, not a deliverable, and much broader than the more specific answer of Concept Model. BABOK 10.18.1

The case study does provide “a list of terms in a particular domain with definitions for those terms and their common synonyms” as a Glossary should do. It includes much more than definitions, though, and better matches the description of a Concept Model. BABOK 10.23.3, 10.11.2

This could be chosen, but the rules and choices are not complex enough to warrant it in this case. “A decision tree is a method of assessing the preferred outcome where multiple sources of uncertainty may exist.” BABOK 10.16.3.3, 10.17.3
Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

A CRUD Matrix would not be suitable because those matrices show “which user roles have permission to access specific information entities“ and “…can be used to show which processes, instead of users, have the create, read, update and delete rights.” BABOK Glossary

Given the list of statement options and the combinations, a table could clearly show when a resulting fee would be charged. “Decision tables generally contain one or more condition columns that map to specific data elements, as well as one or more action or outcome columns.” BABOK 10.17.3.1

Agile projects need engaged stakeholders (product owner), which requires more availability than Daniel has. This approach does not remove his roadblock and is not apt to get his buy-in. "

Negotiation is needed to reach a common understanding, in this case about which has higher priority, the project or operational work. In addition, this kind of a meeting facilitates stakeholder communication and promotes stakeholder support of the proposed changes (BABOK 9.5 introduction).

This is close, but divides by the $200,000 development costs instead of the total cost of $275,000. ((80000+110000+180000) - 275000) / 200000 = .48

The formula in the BABOK for Return on Investment is “((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment).” The numbers in the scenario are: ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) / 275000, which equals .35. Please note the BABOK has some inconsistency in how they define ROI. Their formula above differs from their word explanation, which is: “the net benefits divided by the cost of the change.” “Cost of the change” could be construed as just costs to create and implement a solution, but “Cost of the Investment” would include ongoing costs to support a solution (Total Cost of Ownership), so we suggest using the formula to calculate ROI. BABOK 10.20.3.5 and 8.5.6

content: Option B, since it has a higher ROI than A
feedback: Option B has a .19 ROI compared with option A's .18. The BABOK formula to calculate ROI is: Return on Investment = ((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment). Net benefits are benefits-costs captured in the "Cumulative net" rows, A = $225,000, B = $175,000. Cost of investment is considered to include Total Cost of Ownership and A = $1,275,000 and B = $900,000. Dividing the net benefits by the total investment cost yields ROI of .18 for A and .19 for B. While the increased market share is a business objective, one could assume this is the correct answer. However, the BABOK has some ambiguity when it comes to the similarity of business goals/objectives and business requirements. The BABOK defines business requirements as “statements of goals, objectives, and outcomes that describe why a change has been initiated.” The desire for increased market share clearly reflects that definition, making business requirement and not business objective the correct answer. BABOK 6.2.8 and 2.3
Business goals are broad states or conditions that organizations strive to achieve, often stated qualitatively. The more specific 15% quantitative measure disqualifies the answer. BABOK Glossary

Business requirements are related to business goals and objectives given they are “statements of goals, objectives, and outcomes that describe why a change has been initiated.” The desire for increased market share clearly reflects that definition. BABOK 6.1.8 and 2.3

content: It is part of the Future State Description
feedback: The increased market share is an objective. It does not match the description of the Future State which contains “boundaries of the proposed new, removed, and modified components of the enterprise and the potential value expected from the future state.” BABOK 6.2.8

content: Sequence Diagrams because it can show how the various objects interact in the given scenarios.
feedback: Sequence diagrams are more detailed and specific to objects than what the question is asking for. Use cases are more fundamental and would be a first choice. “A sequence diagram shows how processes or objects interact during a scenario. The classes required to execute the scenario and the messages they pass to one another (triggered by steps in the use case) are displayed on the diagram.” BABOK 10.42.2

content: State Diagrams because the sale goes through numerous states from “for sale” to “sold.”
feedback: A state diagram would probably be helpful to understand the nuances of a sale. However, this answer focuses on just one entity, Sale, and there are multiple processes to be specified in the case study. BABOK 10.44.1

content: Swim Lane Models because the process steps have numerous handoffs between actors.
feedback: It is true there are multiple actors in the case study. Use cases would be more appropriate because of the many interactions directly between the new system and the actors. Swim lanes would be more helpful if the interactions were directly between actors. BABOK 10.35.3.1

content: Use Cases because of the numerous interactions between actors and the system.
feedback: The details presented in the case suggest detailed interactions between the actors in the case and the solution being built. BABOK 10.47.3.2

content: Identify that the more complex requirements are a Derive type of relationship to the requirements for the Make Offer process.
feedback: The new requirements are traceable back to the business need and therefore are valid. It is “used when a requirement is derived from another requirement. This type of relationship is appropriate to link the requirements on different levels of abstraction.” The new Save Offers is a subset of Make Offer and could be modeled as an alternate path from the main scenario. BABOK 5.1.4.2

content: Identify the new requirements as a Depends relationship between the newly discovered Save Offers and the main Make Offer process.
feedback: It is true that Save Offers is somewhat dependent on Make Offer. But, the new requirements are not so much dependent on the current Make Offer process as they are a decomposition of them. Derive is a better relationship. BABOK 5.1.4.2

content: Given the complexity of the newly discovered process, reject the new requirements as being out of scope.
feedback: The business analyst does not reject requirements. If anything, the new requirements would be assessed as a potential change with an assessment and “recommendation to approve, modify, or deny a proposed change to requirements.” BABOK 5.4.1 and 5.4.8

content: Given that Save Offers does not accomplish the desired goal of selling a property, model it as an Exception Path in the Make Offer process.
feedback: The newly discovered Save Offers is actually an Alternate Path of Make Offer, not an exception. Saving an offer might still enable accomplishing the goal of selling a property. “Exception flows describe the desired response by the solution when the goal is unachievable and the use case cannot be successfully completed.” BABOK 10.47.3.2

Teamwork (BABOK 9.5.3) is a useful competency, but it is not a technique.

Brainstorming is useful in a collaborative way to decide on a solution, not what to do once the solution is implemented. (BABOK 10.5).

Process models, both business and technical, describe the flow of activities, or what people do. Process models will be useful to help stakeholders understand the gap between what they do today and what will change with the implementation of the software. (BABOK 10.35).

content: There is no longer a need to send paper catalogues to customers. Our website displays all the items in a catalog and most customers prefer to order online.
feedback: A need is a problem or opportunity to be addressed (BABOK Core Concept Model). This response has multiple reasons why it is not the best one. We do not know what problem we're solving nor what opportunity to seize.We do not know why there is no longer a need to send out paper catalogs. What about customers who do not prefer to order online (what does "most customers" mean)?How do we know that most customers prefer to order online? Is it an assumption? Have we surveyed them? Have we gotten requests to remove them from the paper catalog list?

feedback: The issue is not an issue of conflict, but rather priorities. The BA needs to influence a positive outcome, which is best accomplished by aligning individual goals with organizational goals. BABOK Underlying Competencies 9.5.2.2 (Leadership and Influencing).

content: Meet individually to determine their individual goals and to assist them in understanding how the solution aligns with the organization's objectives.

content: Since the potential value is so high ($2 million or more), have the sponsor (Ben) explain why the decision was made and help them understand how it supports the organization's objectives.
feedback: It's great to involve the sponsor, but we don't have enough information to know whether or not the stakeholders are aware of why the decision was made. The BABOK emphasizes that the BA needs to influence a positive outcome. Having the sponsor explain why the decision was made does not guarantee such an outcome. BABOK Underlying Competencies 9.5.2.2 (Leadership and Influencing).

While the increased market share is a business objective, one could assume this is the correct answer. However, the BABOK has some ambiguity when it comes to the similarity of business goals/objectives and business requirements. The BABOK defines business requirements as “statements of goals, objectives, and outcomes that describe why a change has been initiated.” The desire for increased market share clearly reflects that definition, making business requirement and not business objective the correct answer. BABOK 6.2.8 and 2.3

feedback: The increased market share is an objective. It does not match the description of the Future State which contains “boundaries of the proposed new, removed, and modified components of the enterprise and the potential value expected from the future state.” BABOK 6.2.8
Business goals are broad states or conditions that organizations strive to achieve, often stated qualitatively. The more specific 15% quantitative measure disqualifies the answer. BABOK Glossary

content: Use Cases because of the numerous interactions between actors and the system.

content: State Diagrams because the sale goes through numerous states from “for sale” to “sold.”
feedback: A state diagram would probably be helpful to understand the nuances of a sale. However, this answer focuses on just one entity, Sale, and there are multiple processes to be specified in the case study. BABOK 10.44.1

content: Swim Lane Models because the process steps have numerous handoffs between actors.
feedback: It is true there are multiple actors in the case study. Use cases would be more appropriate because of the many interactions directly between the new system and the actors. Swim lanes would be more helpful if the interactions were directly between actors. BABOK 10.35.3.1

content: Sequence Diagrams because it can show how the various objects interact in the given scenarios.
feedback: Sequence diagrams are more detailed and specific to objects than what the question is asking for. Use cases are more fundamental and would be a first choice. “A sequence diagram shows how processes or objects interact during a scenario. The classes required to execute the scenario and the messages they pass to one another (triggered by steps in the use case) are displayed on the diagram.” BABOK 10.42.2

content: Identify the new requirements as a Depends relationship between the newly discovered Save Offers and the main Make Offer process.
feedback: It is true that Save Offers is somewhat dependent on Make Offer. But, the new requirements are not so much dependent on the current Make Offer process as they are a decomposition of them. Derive is a better relationship. BABOK 5.1.4.2

content: Given that Save Offers does not accomplish the desired goal of selling a property, model it as an Exception Path in the Make Offer process.
feedback: The newly discovered Save Offers is actually an Alternate Path of Make Offer, not an exception. Saving an offer might still enable accomplishing the goal of selling a property. “Exception flows describe the desired response by the solution when the goal is unachievable and the use case cannot be successfully completed.” BABOK 10.47.3.2

content: Identify that the more complex requirements are a Derive type of relationship to the requirements for the Make Offer process.
feedback: The new requirements are traceable back to the business need and therefore are valid. It is “used when a requirement is derived from another requirement. This type of relationship is appropriate to link the requirements on different levels of abstraction.” The new Save Offers is a subset of Make Offer and could be modeled as an alternate path from the main scenario. BABOK 5.1.4.2

content: Given the complexity of the newly discovered process, reject the new requirements as being out of scope.
feedback: The business analyst does not reject requirements. If anything, the new requirements would be assessed as a potential change with an assessment and “recommendation to approve, modify, or deny a proposed change to requirements.” BABOK 5.4.1 and 5.4.8

Measuring them is not the best answer. BABOK 8.1.

content: Option B, since it has a higher ROI than A
feedback: Option B has a .19 ROI compared with option A's .18. The BABOK formula to calculate ROI is: Return on Investment = ((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment). Net benefits are benefits-costs captured in the "Cumulative net" rows, A = $225,000, B = $175,000. Cost of investment is considered to include Total Cost of Ownership and A = $1,275,000 and B = $900,000. Dividing the net benefits by the total investment cost yields ROI of .18 for A and .19 for B. BABOK 10.20.3.5

To provide needed flexibility in managing accounts and statements, a data model is essential for structuring the data needed to accomplish it. Given the business rules and relationships presented in the case study, a data model is needed to capture them. “It visually represents the elements that are important to the business, the attributes associated with those elements, and the significant relationships among them. Data models are frequently used in elicitation and requirements analysis and Design.” BABOK 10.15.2

User stories are used “to define features of value to a stakeholder using short, simple documentation. They can serve as a basis for identifying needs and allow for the prioritizing, estimating, and planning of solutions.” Given the solution features are already known, these would not be as helpful for modeling the requirements for the features. BABOK 10.48.2

A State Model shows the various states and transformations that an entity might go through like an Account or Statement. It focuses on one entity at a time, and the case study refers to multiple entities: Customer, Account, Location, and Statement. A Data Model would tie all of those together. BABOK 10.44.2

This is a potentially useful tool that “illustrates the movement and transformation of data between externals (entities) and processes.” It does not depict the relationships of one entity to another, though, so cannot capture the business rules like a data model can. A flexible data model is required for the DFD to reference, so this answer is not the best. BABOK 10.13.2

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Concept models can be effective where:… the initiative needs to capture large numbers of business rules…” BABOK 10.11.2

Very close answer, since there is similarity between a Concept Model and Data Dictionary. The latter typically includes data-specific details that are not present in the case study. “A data dictionary is used to document standard definitions of data elements, their meanings, and allowable values. A data dictionary contains definitions of each data element and indicates how those elements combine into composite data elements.” BABOK 10.12.2

It is true that if there is documentation available to review like in the case study, reviewing it involves document analysis. It is a technique, not a deliverable, and much broader than the more specific answer of Concept Model. BABOK 10.18.1

The case study does provide “a list of terms in a particular domain with definitions for those terms and their common synonyms” as a Glossary should do. It includes much more than definitions, though, and better matches the description of a Concept Model. BABOK 10.23.3, 10.11.2

This could be chosen, but the rules and choices are not complex enough to warrant it in this case. “A decision tree is a method of assessing the preferred outcome where multiple sources of uncertainty may exist.” BABOK 10.16.3.3, 10.17.3
Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

Given the list of statement options and the combinations, a table could clearly show when a resulting fee would be charged. “Decision tables generally contain one or more condition columns that map to specific data elements, as well as one or more action or outcome columns.” BABOK 10.17.3.1

A CRUD Matrix would not be suitable because those matrices show “which user roles have permission to access specific information entities“ and “…can be used to show which processes, instead of users, have the create, read, update and delete rights.” BABOK Glossary
A focus group can elicit thoughts and feelings about an issue in a group setting, such as the statement limitations. It would be a likely way Newbank discovered its customers’ needs. 10.21.1

content: Document Analysis by analyzing complaint logs
feedback: It is true that complaints surfaced and they likely were logged. Data Mining may have uncovered patterns that suggested new statement options. But, document analysis involves “researching existing solutions to validate how those solutions are currently implemented.” Solution documentation would probably not reveal customer needs. BABOK 10.18.2
The case said the new statement options give Newbank a competitive advantage. They likely discovered that after they found out their customers’ needs using other methods. 10.4.2

feedback: This is an invented variation on Compromise, where no one gets everything they want, but where they have input and get some of the things that are important to them. content: Talk to the sponsor, since the sponsor makes the ultimate decision about priorities. Set up a meeting to communicate the sponsor's decision to the affected stakeholders. This is close, but divides by the $200,000 development costs instead of the total cost of $275,000. ((80000+110000+180000) - 275000) / 200000 = .48

The formula in the BABOK for Return on Investment is “((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment).” The numbers in the scenario are: ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) / 275000, which equals .35. Please note the BABOK has some inconsistency in how they define ROI. Their formula above differs from their word explanation, which is: “the net benefits divided by the cost of the change.” “Cost of the change” could be construed as just costs to create and implement a solution, but “Cost of the Investment” would include ongoing costs to support a solution (Total Cost of Ownership), so we suggest using the formula to calculate ROI. BABOK 10.20.3.5 and 8.5.6

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

content: The rate which an investment breaks even. It tells whether the proposed solution is worth investing in.
feedback: This answer is an explanation for Internal Rate of Return. "the interest rate at which an investment breaks even, and is usually used to determine if the change, solution or solution approach is worth investing in."

content: A ratio of the benefits of a solution to be realized compared with the cost to obtain them. It provides a relative value of a change and can be compared with other potential investments.
feedback: "The return on investment (ROI) of a planned change is expressed as a percentage measuring the net benefits divided by the cost of the change." BABOK 10.20.3.5

content: It is an assumed rate used in present value calculations. It projects a rate the organization could earn compared to what it could earn on other investments.
feedback:
This answer is the explanation for Discount Rate. "The discount rate is the assumed interest rate used in present value calculations. In
general, this is similar to the interest rate that the organization would expect to earn if it invested its money elsewhere."

content: A ratio of the present value of the benefits minus the original cost of the investment. It represents whether the project would yield a higher return than investments elsewhere.
feedback: This answer is an explanation for Net Present Value. "... the present value of the benefits minus the original cost of the investment."

content: Either, since they have the same ROI
feedback: Option A has a higher overall cumulative net amount, but its ROI is less than option B.

content: Option B, since it has a higher ROI than A
feedback: Option B has a .19 ROI compared with option A's .18. The BABOK formula to calculate ROI is: Return on Investment = ((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment). Net benefits are benefits-costs captured in the "Cumulative net" rows, A = $225,000, B = $175,000. Cost of investment is considered to include Total Cost of Ownership and A = $1,275,000 and B = $900,000. Dividing the net benefits by the total investment cost yields ROI of .18 for A and .19 for B. BABOK 10.20.3.5

content: Neither, since both have a negative ROI
feedback: True, both options have some years in which the net benefits are negative. However, the BABOK formula divides the net overall benefits by the total investment cost, which yields positive ROI of .18 for A and .19 for B. BABOK 10.20.3.5

content: Option A, since it has a higher ROI than B

This answer represents the net of the benefits minus the expected costs. ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) = 95,000. It is only part of the calculation for the Return on Investment.

The formula in the BABOK for Return on Investment is “((Total Benefits – Cost of the Investment) / Cost of the Investment).” The numbers in the scenario are: ((80000+110000+180000)-275000) / 275000, which equals .35. Please note the BABOK has some inconsistency in how they define ROI. Their formula above differs from their word explanation, which is: “the net benefits divided by the cost of the change.” “Cost of the change” could be construed as just costs to create and implement a solution, but “Cost of the Investment” would include ongoing costs to support a solution (Total Cost of Ownership), so we suggest using the formula to calculate ROI. BABOK 10.20.3.5 and 8.5.6
This answer omits the $75,000 installation cost in the benefits minus cost. ((80000+110000+180000)-200,000) / 275,000 = .62, which is incorrect.

To provide needed flexibility in managing accounts and statements, a data model is essential for structuring the data needed to accomplish it. Given the business rules and relationships presented in the case study, a data model is needed to capture them. “It visually represents the elements that are important to the business, the attributes associated with those elements, and the significant relationships among them. Data models are frequently used in elicitation and requirements analysis and Design.” BABOK 10.15.2

User stories are used “to define features of value to a stakeholder using short, simple documentation. They can serve as a basis for identifying needs and allow for the prioritizing, estimating, and planning of solutions.” Given the solution features are already known, these would not be as helpful for modeling the requirements for the features. BABOK 10.48.2

A State Model shows the various states and transformations that an entity might go through like an Account or Statement. It focuses on one entity at a time, and the case study refers to multiple entities: Customer, Account, Location, and Statement. A Data Model would tie all of those together. BABOK 10.44.2

This is a potentially useful tool that “illustrates the movement and transformation of data between externals (entities) and processes.” It does not depict the relationships of one entity to another, though, so cannot capture the business rules like a data model can. A flexible data model is required for the DFD to reference, so this answer is not the best. BABOK 10.13.2

The case study does provide “a list of terms in a particular domain with definitions for those terms and their common synonyms” as a Glossary should do. It includes much more than definitions, though, and better matches the description of a Concept Model. BABOK 10.23.3, 10.11.2

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Concept models can be effective where:… the initiative needs to capture large numbers of business rules…” BABOK 10.11.2

Very close answer, since there is similarity between a Concept Model and Data Dictionary. The latter typically includes data-specific details that are not present in the case study. “A data dictionary is used to document standard definitions of data elements, their meanings, and allowable values. A data dictionary contains definitions of each data element and indicates how those elements combine into composite data elements.” BABOK 10.12.2

It is true that if there is documentation available to review like in the case study, reviewing it involves document analysis. It is a technique, not a deliverable, and much broader than the more specific answer of Concept Model. BABOK 10.18.1

A CRUD Matrix would not be suitable because those matrices show “which user roles have permission to access specific information entities“ and “…can be used to show which processes, instead of users, have the create, read, update and delete rights.” BABOK Glossary
Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

To provide needed flexibility in managing accounts and statements, a data model is essential for structuring the data needed to accomplish it. Given the business rules and relationships presented in the case study, a data model is needed to capture them. “It visually represents the elements that are important to the business, the attributes associated with those elements, and the significant relationships among them. Data models are frequently used in elicitation and requirements analysis and Design.” BABOK 10.15.2

User stories are used “to define features of value to a stakeholder using short, simple documentation. They can serve as a basis for identifying needs and allow for the prioritizing, estimating, and planning of solutions.” Given the solution features are already known, these would not be as helpful for modeling the requirements for the features. BABOK 10.48.2

A State Model shows the various states and transformations that an entity might go through like an Account or Statement. It focuses on one entity at a time, and the case study refers to multiple entities: Customer, Account, Location, and Statement. A Data Model would tie all of those together. BABOK 10.44.2

This is a potentially useful tool that “illustrates the movement and transformation of data between externals (entities) and processes.” It does not depict the relationships of one entity to another, though, so cannot capture the business rules like a data model can. A flexible data model is required for the DFD to reference, so this answer is not the best. BABOK 10.13.2

The case study does provide “a list of terms in a particular domain with definitions for those terms and their common synonyms” as a Glossary should do. It includes much more than definitions, though, and better matches the description of a Concept Model. BABOK 10.23.3, 10.11.2

A “concept model starts with a glossary, which typically focuses on the core noun concepts of a domain.” The case study offers more than definitions, including several rules. “It is especially important where high precision and subtle distinctions need to be made. Concept models can be effective where:… the initiative needs to capture large numbers of business rules…” BABOK 10.11.2

Very close answer, since there is similarity between a Concept Model and Data Dictionary. The latter typically includes data-specific details that are not present in the case study. “A data dictionary is used to document standard definitions of data elements, their meanings, and allowable values. A data dictionary contains definitions of each data element and indicates how those elements combine into composite data elements.” BABOK 10.12.2

It is true that if there is documentation available to review like in the case study, reviewing it involves document analysis. It is a technique, not a deliverable, and much broader than the more specific answer of Concept Model. BABOK 10.18.1

A CRUD Matrix would not be suitable because those matrices show “which user roles have permission to access specific information entities“ and “…can be used to show which processes, instead of users, have the create, read, update and delete rights.” BABOK Glossary
Data Dictionaries are used to define data, give examples, and document things like metadata so this would not be the best answer. 10.12.2

Downtime relates to operational efficiency, not regulatory compliance.

content: Interpret and synthesize the performance measures already reported to analyze the value of the current solution before replacing it.
feedback: There are several solution performance measurements already in the case study. It could be argued that additional measurements are needed, but the analysis should point out if additional measurements are needed. BABOK 8.2.

content: Determine the measures that can be used to evaluate the effectiveness of the software package the CEO wants.
feedback: Rather than “jumping to a solution” which is what the CEO is doing, the BABOK is clear that current solution performance should be measured. Part of that measurement is to assess “how it aligns with enterprise goals and objectives.” The measurements already cited in the case reflect that the current “solution” in place does not support the company’s goals and objectives. Measuring them is not the best answer. BABOK 8.1.

content: Estimate the potential value for the PAS option to establish whether it is appropriate to meet the company's needs.

content: Develop a change strategy to justify why the software package is the best approach.
feedback: The BABOK mentions that change strategies describe the nature of a change when the current and future states have been defined. However, the CEO's desire for a software package represents stated requirements more than a future state. BABOK 6.4.2

content: The BA should define the business need and assess the current state before evaluating any solutions.

content: Talk to the sponsor, since the sponsor makes the ultimate decision about priorities. Set up a meeting to communicate the sponsor's decision to the affected stakeholders. This conflict-resolution technique is used when the initiative is stuck when there are tight timeframes, and when not having a decision would delay the initiative. The intent is to ensure that development of the solution is not delayed and that all parties can move forward.
feedback: Forcing a decision will resolve the conflict, but it does not identify underlying interests of all the parties and will not strengthen relationships among the stakeholders. The BABOK does not recognize forcing as an effective conflict resolution technique. BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills.

content: Talk to the stakeholders individually. Ensure that you understand the real need behind the stated need. Have a meeting with the sponsor to discuss these needs. Encourage the sponsor to talk to the stakeholders and get them on board. The intent is to ensure all parties are heard.
feedback: The BABOK does not recognize this as an effective technique. BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills. A key goal of negotiation and resolving conflict is to ensure that it strengthens the relationships among the different stakeholders. This process does not accomplish that goal.

content: Facilitate several meetings to discuss the differences among the stakeholders, ensure that they have an opportunity to express their feelings and needs, ensure an outcome that satisfies the needs of the stakeholder and aligns with the business need. The intent is to strengthen the relationships among the affected stakeholders.
feedback: BABOK 9.5.4.3, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution Skills suggest that it will probably take multiple meetings to have all parties be heard and have their feelings and needs expressed.

content: Facilitate a meeting. List all the needs on a board. Everyone gets to choose their top two priorities. After the other needs have been eliminated, each stakeholder votes on their top choice. The intent is to ensure all parties are heard and that they have input into the outcome. The business analyst ensures that the outcome aligns with the business need.
feedback: This is an invented variation on Compromise, where no one gets everything they want, but where they have input and get some of the things that are important to them. Capacity is 80,000 subscribers given that 20,000 = 25% of capacity. (20,000 * 4 = 80,000). Year 1: 20,000 + 10,000 (50%) growth = 30,000. Year 2: 30,000 + 15,000 = 45,000. Year 3: 45,000 + 22,500 = 67,500. Year 4: 67,500 + 33,750 = 101,250. (Note: you can also multiply each year's subscriber total times 1.5 to calculate 50% growth.) The subscriber total exceeds the 80,000 capacity sometime in year 4, making this the correct answer. Capacity is part of "Scalability," an element of Non-Functional Requirements. BABOK 10.30.3.1

content: Identify the appropriate decision makers and process needed for decisions to be made.
feedback: This is a partial description of the task Plan Business Analysis Governance, which uses the BA Approach and Stakeholder Engagement Approach as inputs.
